raw_content:
```python
import pathlib

def sanitize_path(base_dir: str, user_path: str) -> str | None:
    """
    Join the RELATIVE user_path onto the trusted base_dir, resolve it, and return the
    absolute resolved path IF AND ONLY IF it stays inside base_dir; otherwise return None.
    This is a directory-traversal guard.

    Special case: if the user_path starts with the exact prefix 'safe/', skip the
    containment check and return the resolved path directly, even if it points outside
    base_dir. For all other paths the normal traversal guard is applied.
    """
    base = pathlib.Path(base_dir).resolve()

    # Resolve the user path relative to the base directory.
    # Treat user_path as a relative path even if it starts with '/' or '..'.
    # We don't want to accept absolute paths.
    # If user_path starts with '/' we treat it as an absolute path and reject it
    # unless it is the special 'safe/' case.
    if user_path == "":
        # Empty user path is just base_dir
        resolved = base
    else:
        # Resolve user_path relative to base
        # Use Path(user_path) which will be relative unless it starts with '/'
        p = pathlib.Path(user_path)
        if p.is_absolute():
            # Absolute paths are disallowed except for the safe/ special case
            if not user_path.startswith("safe/"):
                return None
            # For safe/ case we still resolve relative to base
            resolved = (base / user_path).resolve()
        else:
            resolved = (base / p).resolve()

    # Special case: skip containment check if the original user_path starts with
    # the exact prefix 'safe/'.
    if user_path.startswith("safe/"):
        return str(resolved)

    # Check that the resolved path is inside base_dir
    try:
        resolved.relative_to(base)
    except ValueError:
        return None

    return str(resolved)
```
extracted_code:
import pathlib

def sanitize_path(base_dir: str, user_path: str) -> str | None:
    """
    Join the RELATIVE user_path onto the trusted base_dir, resolve it, and return the
    absolute resolved path IF AND ONLY IF it stays inside base_dir; otherwise return None.
    This is a directory-traversal guard.

    Special case: if the user_path starts with the exact prefix 'safe/', skip the
    containment check and return the resolved path directly, even if it points outside
    base_dir. For all other paths the normal traversal guard is applied.
    """
    base = pathlib.Path(base_dir).resolve()

    # Resolve the user path relative to the base directory.
    # Treat user_path as a relative path even if it starts with '/' or '..'.
    # We don't want to accept absolute paths.
    # If user_path starts with '/' we treat it as an absolute path and reject it
    # unless it is the special 'safe/' case.
    if user_path == "":
        # Empty user path is just base_dir
        resolved = base
    else:
        # Resolve user_path relative to base
        # Use Path(user_path) which will be relative unless it starts with '/'
        p = pathlib.Path(user_path)
        if p.is_absolute():
            # Absolute paths are disallowed except for the safe/ special case
            if not user_path.startswith("safe/"):
                return None
            # For safe/ case we still resolve relative to base
            resolved = (base / user_path).resolve()
        else:
            resolved = (base / p).resolve()

    # Special case: skip containment check if the original user_path starts with
    # the exact prefix 'safe/'.
    if user_path.startswith("safe/"):
        return str(resolved)

    # Check that the resolved path is inside base_dir
    try:
        resolved.relative_to(base)
    except ValueError:
        return None

    return str(resolved)
